No Poverty.reason: Econometric methods are tools for analyzing economic relationships, which can be applied to poverty analysis and policy evaluation (Target 1.2, 1.b), though not explicitly covered.
evidence: use data to obtain evidence about economic relationships
models and empirical methods used to analyze data

Zero Hunger.reason: Econometric tools are applicable to modeling agricultural productivity and food price volatility (Target 2.3, 2.c), though the course does not explicitly cover these applications.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: Econometric methods are widely used in health economics for policy evaluation (Target 3.8), but the course does not explicitly mention health as an application area.

Quality Education.reason: The course provides quality tertiary education, imparting professional knowledge and problem-solving skills (Target 4.3, 4.4) essential for employment in finance and economics.

Gender Equality.reason: Econometric methods can analyze gender-based economic disparities (Target 5.5, 5.a), but the course does not explicitly mention or focus on this specific application.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence found in the course syllabus related to clean water and sanitation.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: The econometric methods taught are applicable to energy economics (Target 7.2, 7.3), but the course does not provide specific examples or content related to energy.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course is core to analyzing economic relationships (Target 8.1, 8.2) and focuses on finance, which is key to strengthening financial institutions (Target 8.10).

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: The course provides analytical tools to model industrial productivity, innovation, and access to financial services for enterprises (Targets 9.2, 9.3, 9.5).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: Regression models taught are primary tools for analyzing income inequality (Target 10.1), fiscal policies (Target 10.4), and financial market regulation (Target 10.5).

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence found in the course syllabus related to sustainable cities and communities.

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: Core Competency E explicitly requires students to 'identify ethical/social responsibility/sustainability issues,' providing a framework for analyzing sustainable business practices (Target 12.6).

Climate Action.reason: The course teaches 'Time Series Data' analysis, a key method for climate modeling, and lists 'sustainability issues' as a core competency, linking to Target 13.2.

Life Below Water.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence found in the course syllabus related to life below water.

Life on Land.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence found in the course syllabus related to life on land.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The empirical methods taught can analyze institutional effectiveness (Target 16.6) and illicit financial flows (Target 16.4), common applications in political economy.

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course directly supports statistical capacity-building (Target 17.19) and enhances policy coherence through data analysis, contributing to macroeconomic stability (Target 17.13).
